skill.name: brainstorming
skill.description: Use before creative or constructive work (features, architecture, behavior). Transforms vague ideas into validated designs through disciplined reasoning and collaboration.
skill.summary: Transform vague ideas into validated designs through structured dialogue before implementation begins.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 25 static findings are false positives from pattern matching on English documentation text. The skill contains no executable code, only structured workflow instructions for design facilitation. Flagged patterns like 'algorithm', 'lock', and 'agent' appear in benign context (design process descriptions, workflow stage names, skill references). No security risks present.

content.value_statement: Prevents premature implementation and hidden assumptions by enforcing structured design dialogue. Transforms vague concepts into clear, validated specifications through disciplined reasoning before coding begins.
actual_capabilities: Guides structured dialogue to extract and clarify requirements before implementation
Enforces mandatory understanding lock with confirmation before design phases
Facilitates exploration of multiple design approaches with trade-off analysis
Maintains decision logs documenting alternatives and rationale for choices
Breaks complex designs into incremental sections for validation
Generates design documentation with assumptions, constraints, and decisions
limitations: Does not write code or implement features - focuses only on design phase
Requires active user participation and confirmation at each stage
Works best for feature, architecture, and behavior design not suited for bug fixes
Cannot proceed to implementation until all exit criteria are explicitly met

use_cases.title: New Feature Design
use_cases.description: Product teams use this skill to transform feature ideas into validated technical designs before development begins, ensuring alignment on requirements and constraints.

use_cases.title: System Architecture Planning
use_cases.description: Architects apply structured brainstorming to explore multiple architectural approaches, document trade-offs, and validate design decisions with stakeholders.

use_cases.title: Requirements Clarification
use_cases.description: Developers use this workflow to clarify vague requirements, identify hidden assumptions, and establish success criteria before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Design Session
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to add a user authentication system to my web application. Can you help me think through the design?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new feature design with a high-level idea

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to decide between microservices and monolithic architecture for my e-commerce platform. Help me explore the trade-offs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing multiple architectural approaches for a system

prompt_templates.title: Constraint Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: We are planning a real-time collaboration feature. What constraints should we consider for performance, scale, and reliability?

prompt_templates.title: Decision Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: We decided to use PostgreSQL for our data layer. Please document this decision with the alternatives we considered and the rationale.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a formal decision log entry for an architectural choice

output_examples.input: I want to add file upload functionality
output: - Building: File upload feature for user documents
- Purpose: Allow users to share documents in collaborative projects
- Constraints: Max 50MB per file, PDF and Office formats only
- Non-goals: No video processing, no real-time preview
- Files stored in S3 with CDN delivery
- Retention policy for deleted user accounts?

output_examples.input: How should we handle API rate limiting?
output: Design Approaches for API Rate Limiting:
- Pros: Bursts allowed, smooth throttling, industry standard
- Cons: Requires Redis for distributed systems
- Use when: Consistent experience matters more than simplicity
- Cons: Bursty at window boundaries, unfair to new users
- Use when: Basic protection is sufficient, simplicity prioritized
- Cons: High memory usage, complex implementation
- Use when: Precision is critical, resources available

best_practices: Answer one question at a time to maintain focused dialogue and prevent overwhelming discussions
Confirm understanding lock explicitly before moving to design phases to prevent rework
Document all assumptions and constraints rather than leaving them implicit
Explore multiple design alternatives rather than committing to the first idea
Use decision logs to preserve rationale for future maintainers
anti_patterns: Skipping the understanding lock to rush into implementation details prematurely
Asking multiple questions at once which creates confusion and incomplete answers
Making silent assumptions about constraints, scale, or requirements without validation
Proposing solutions before understanding the core problem and user needs
Ignoring non-functional requirements like performance, security, and reliability

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use this skill before starting any creative or constructive work including features, architecture changes, or behavior modifications. It is most valuable when requirements are vague or the design has high impact.

faq.question: Can this skill write code for me?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses exclusively on design and planning. It explicitly prevents implementation until the design is validated, documented, and all exit criteria are met.

faq.question: What is the understanding lock?
faq.answer: The understanding lock is a mandatory checkpoint where you confirm that the AI accurately understands your requirements, constraints, goals, and non-goals before any design work begins.

faq.question: Why does this skill ask questions one at a time?
faq.answer: Single questions prevent overwhelming dialogue, ensure each topic gets full attention, and allow you to correct misunderstandings before they compound into larger issues.

faq.question: What happens after the design is complete?
faq.answer: The skill generates formal documentation including the understanding summary, assumptions, decision log, and final design. You can then choose to proceed with implementation separately.

faq.question: Can I use this for small changes or bug fixes?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for feature, architecture, and behavior work. Small bug fixes typically do not require the full structured design process.
